paper_title: AsyncWebRL: Efficient Multi-Step RL for Visual Web Agents

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Training vision-language web agents with multi-step RL is compute-intensive, with two dominant forms of inefficiency: idle GPUs in synchronous RL, and trajectories that use more steps and tokens than necessary. We present AsyncWebRL, which addresses both. On the system side, an asynchronous design overlaps rollout, gradient update, and policy refresh across iterations, paired with two web-agent-specific adaptations, namely an everlasting rollout pool and lightweight screenshot handling, that together deliver up to a $2.9\times$ end-to-end training-throughput speedup over the previously fastest open synchronous pipeline (WebGym). On the algorithmic side, we identify the per-trajectory normalizer $1/|τ_i|$ in multi-step GRPO as the root cause of trajectory-level and token-level inefficiency: because failures are systematically longer than successes, it down-weights the negative gradient on failed tokens, so the policy keeps producing verbose memory schemas. Replacing $1/|τ_i|$ with a constant $1/k$ breaks this coupling, contracting trajectories while preserving aggregate success. Together, these contributions set a new open-source state of the art on the WebGym out-of-distribution test split (+5.8% relative over the 42.9% prior best), with the largest gains on the harder slices (+42% relative on Medium, +48% relative on Hard).

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Training vision-language web agents with multi-step RL is compute-intensive, with two dominant forms of inefficiency: idle GPUs in synchronous RL, and trajectories that use more steps and tokens than necessary. We present AsyncWebRL, which addresses both. On the system side, an asynchronous design overlaps rollout, gradient update, and policy refresh across iterations, paired with two webagent-specific adaptations, namely an everlasting rollout pool and lightweight screenshot handling, that together deliver up to 2.9 end-toend training-throughput speedup over the previously fastest open synchronous pipeline (WebGym). On the algorithmic side, we identify the per-trajectory normalizer 1/τi in multi-step GRPO as the root cause of trajectory-level and token-level inefficiency: because failures are systematically longer than successes, it downweights the negative gradient on failed tokens, so the policy keeps producing verbose memory schemas. Replacing 1/τi with constant 1/k breaks this coupling, contracting trajectories while preserving aggregate success. Together, these contributions set new open-source state of the art on the WebGym out-of-distribution test split (+5.8% relative over the 42.9% prior best), with the largest gains on the harder slices (+42% relative on Medium, +48% relative on Hard).

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Vision-language web agents trained with multistep reinforcement learning have rapidly become leading recipe for autonomous browsers that complete real-world tasks across the long tail of the web (Bai et al., 2026; Wang et al., 2025; Wei et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2026), part of broader shift from single-turn preference tuning to long-horizon, environment-grounded agent training. The defining feature of this regime is compute: each training run consumes hundreds of GPU-hours against hundreds of concurrent browser sessions, and progress is largely function of how many trajectories the trainer can profitably consume per wall-clock hour. Against this backdrop, any compute inefficiency translates directly into lower-performing agent at fixed budget. This paper targets both forms of inefficiency within single framework, combining (1) fully asynchronous system design tailored to per-step visual rollouts with (2) one-line algorithmic fix that contracts trajectory and token usage without harming ending success. The natural systems solution for GPU idleness is async RL, where rollout, gradient update, and policy refresh proceed concurrently across iteration boundaries (Mnih et al., 2016; Espeholt et al., 2018; Hilton et al., 2021). On the surface this appears to be solved problem: single-step LLM-RL is already async, and WebGym (Bai et al., 2026) already makes within-iteration rollouts async. Combining the three properties visual, multi-step, and fully async, however, requires coordinated design that none of the existing open-source systems carry together (Section 2). The core difficulty has two parts. First, the natural payload of visual multi-step rollout, tens of high-resolution screenshots per trajectory shared across workers every step, swamps the standard inter-worker data store and pushes the framework into slow disk-spill path that erases any async benefit. Second, an iteration-synchronous rollout pool of the kind WebGym uses pays warm-up cost on hundreds of browser sessions every round. We address both with two web-agent-specific designs: lightweight screenshot handling and an everlasting rollout pool. The former keeps image tensors out of the shared data store entirely, routing only lightweight references between rollout workers and the trainer. The latter keeps workers continuously alive across iteration boundaries so episodes hand off immediately and parameter updates of πθ never stall collection (Section 3.1). Closing the resulting off-policy gap also requires an algorithmic correction. We adopt the decoupled-PPO factorization of Hilton et al. (2021), splitting the standard πθ/πbehave ratio into rollout-staleness term and current-update term and centering PPO-style clipping (Schulman et al., 2017) on proximal policy πprox πθ. As we will show in Section 3.2 and Section 4.3, this roughly halves the clip-trigger rate and substantially speeds reward improvement. The resulting system is, to our knowledge, the fastest reported open multi-step RL framework for visual web agents, at 2.4 to 2.9 end-to-end speedup over prior open rollout pools (Bai et al., 2026). Beyond this form of off-policy correction, we identify that per-trajectory step-number based normalization1, i.e., 1/τi in multi-step GRPO (Shao et al., 2024) as the root cause of trajectoryand token-level inefficiency, and replace it with constant 1/k (Section 5). The intuition follows Dr. GRPO (Liu et al., 2025b) but at step-level granularity rather than token granularity: in our setting failures average 12.5 steps against 5.1 for successes, so 1/τi attenuates the gradient on failed tokens by 2.4, and the policy responds by producing verbose memory schemas. Combined with the off-policy correction, this sets new state of the art on the WebGym OOD test split (Section 4.2). Our contributions. (1) System. The first open multi-step RL framework for visual web agents that is fully async end-to-end, via an everlasting rollout pool, lightweight screenshot handling, and decoupled importance-sampling ratio (Section 3.1); this delivers 2.4 to 2.9 end-to-end speedup over the previously fastest open sync pipeline and new open-source state of the art on the WebGym OOD test split (+5.8% relative over the 42.9% prior best). (2) Algorithm. diagnosis of the per-trajectory step-number normalizer 1/τi in multi-step GRPO as the root cause of trajectoryand token-level inefficiency, traced through lengthcoupled memory drift (Section 5), and one-line fix replacing 1/τi with constant 1/k that drives the largest gains on the harder OOD slices (+42% relative on Medium, +48% relative on Hard over the prior WebGym SOTA). 1Throughout the paper, step-number normalization refers to the multi-step GRPO factor 1/τi that normalizes the loss by the number of steps (not tokens) in trajectory. This is distinct from Dr. GRPOs removal of the single-turn factor 1/yi, which normalizes by the number of tokens in response. We call the latter token-number normalization.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: System: visual, multi-step, and async RL. Multistep RL on VLM policies has been studied for web browsing (Bai et al., 2026; Wei et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2026), computer use (Bai et al., 2024, 2025; Wang et al., 2025), embodied learning (Kim et al., 2025; Liu et al., 2025a), and sequential decisionmaking (Zhai et al., 2024). Open-source frameworks split into two disjoint subsets that AsyncWebRL bridges. Async LLM-RL on text and async single-turn VLM-RL (Fu et al., 2025; Lu et al., 2025; Sheng et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2025; Han et al., 2025; Zhong et al., 2025; Zhou et al., 2025; Noukhovitch et al., 2024; Sheng et al., 2024) ship at most one image per training example; pushing multi-turn web-agent rollouts (tens of highresolution screenshots per trajectory, hundreds concurrent) through their shared data store exhausts its budget and triggers disk-spill path that erases the async benefit. Sync multi-step VLM-RL (Bai et al., 2026; Wei et al., 2025; Bai et al., 2024, 2025) carries the right workload but resynchronizes at every iteration boundary, leaving GPU bubbles. Switching the latter to fully async execution opens an offpolicy gap that sync frameworks never see: training batches contain tokens drawn from several policy versions at once, and the trust region must be recentered on proximal policy (Hilton et al., 2021) so that clipping reflects current-update movement rather than rollout staleness. The closed-source UITARS-2 (Wang et al., 2025) reportedly satisfies all three properties but releases only model weights. AsyncWebRL is, to our knowledge, the first open framework to combine all three. Algorithm: loss shape under length asymmetry. On the algorithmic side, Dr. GRPO (Liu et al., 2025b) showed that the single-turn GRPO normalizer 1/yi reweights per-token gradients between long and short responses, and removing it (replacing 1/yi with constant) corrects an unintended length bias inside single response. We extend the same diagnosis one level up. In multi-step GRPO (Shao et al., 2024; Zeng et al., 2025), the analogous factor is the per-trajectory step-number normalizer 1/τi, which couples per-token gradient scale to trajectory length. What is new at the step granularity that the single-turn analysis does not see is that the multi-step web-agent setting has two structural features which turn Dr. GRPOs steplevel prediction into self-reinforcing failure mode: failed trajectories are dominated by horizon exhundreds of browser sessions when the rollout pool is rebuilt each round.

sections.title: 3.2 Algorithm
sections.content: Decoupled off-policy correction. Asynchronous execution shifts the sampling distribution: the policy that generated given token (πbehave) is several updates behind the policy πθ the trainer is now updating, and single trajectory in training batch can be stitched from different policy snapshots. The natural correction is importance sampling: weight every token by πθ/πbehave for an unbiased off-policy gradient, and clip this ratio to [1ϵ, 1+ϵ] as in PPO. Under async RL the same ratio must capture two quantities at once: how much the policy has moved since the rollout was sampled (rollout staleness), and how much the optimizer has moved the policy during the current gradient update. Clipping single coupled ratio confounds the two, so rollout staleness alone triggers clip events at many token positions and slows training substantially. We adopt the decoupled-PPO factorization of Hilton et al. (2021) (also used by AReaL (Fu et al., 2025) for text RL) and split the ratio into rollout-staleness factor πprox/πbehave and currentupdate factor πθ/πprox, where πprox is the policy snapshot at the start of the current update. The PPO clip is then centered around πprox only, so that clip events reflect how much the optimizer has moved the policy during this update rather than staleness inherited from the rollout. Empirically this roughly halves the clip rate relative to the coupled formulation (Section 4.3), and enters the final loss as the inner min of Equation (1) (a negative-advantage dual-clip extension is given in Equation (2)). Removing trajectory-length normalization. Separately from the off-policy correction, we change the loss aggregation. The standard multistep GRPO (Shao et al., 2024) loss normalizes each rollout by its own step count τi, which under our setting introduces length-coupled gradient attenuation we trace in Section 5. The fix is one line: replace 1/τi with constant 1/k, where is the Easy-difficulty horizon (10 throughout this paper). That is, instead of every rollout entering the loss with total weight 1 regardless of length, each rollout enters with weight τi/k, restoring full per-token gradient weight on the long failures the policy must learn to avoid. This change appears as the constant 1/k outer factor in Equation (1). We treat each trajectory as bandit and share single trajectory-level advantage ˆAi = (ri Figure 1: Multi-step Asynchronous Management. Compared to WebGym, AsyncWebRL eliminates the inter-iteration bubble time caused by reconstructing the rollout pool at every iteration boundary and waiting for the policy refresh of πt to complete. Colored blocks denote concurrent rollout workers producing trajectories, gradient updates on πt, and policy refreshes that broadcast new weights to the rollout workers. White gaps under synchronous RL (top) are bubble time. AsyncWebRL (bottom) eliminates these gaps by maintaining an everlasting rollout pool so that rollout, gradient update, and policy refresh overlap continuously. haustion rather than clearly wrong actions, and an additive memory prompt against multi-step visual environment mechanically lengthens per-step responses as step count grows. We trace these features in Section 5 and show that the multi-step counterpart of Dr. GRPOs fix breaks the loop at its source.

sections.title: 3.1 System
sections.content: Our goals are to keep GPUs from waiting on each other, and to keep the shared inter-worker store from saturating under per-step image traffic. On top of the synchronous multi-step rollout pool of WebGym (Bai et al., 2026), we make two changes: fully asynchronous execution with an everlasting rollout pool, and lightweight screenshot handling. The first keeps rollout workers continuously alive across iteration boundaries and overlaps rollout, gradient update, and policy refresh end to end: when an episode ends, the next begins immediately on the same worker without waiting for the rest of the batch or for the next iteration. The parameter update of πθ can happen at any time while rollout continues; new weights are broadcast in place to the inference workers and the next rollout segment is sampled under the updated policy (Figure 1). The second (Section B) keeps per-step image tensors out of the shared store and routes only lightweight references between rollout workers and the trainer, which prevents the disk-spill path that hundreds of concurrent high-resolution rollouts otherwise induce. Together these eliminate the GPU idle time spent waiting for the slowest trajectory in batch and the warm-up cost paid on (θ) = Eτ πbehave

With rollouts per task, τi steps per rollout, and τi,j tokens per step, the AsyncWebRL loss combines the two changes above with the standard GRPO surrogate (Equation (1)). The red-colored terms in Equation (1) mark these two changes: the constant 1/k in place of 1/τi, and the decoupled clip centered around πprox with πprox/πbehave as an unclipped weight. Following DAPO (Yu et al., 2025), we also drop the reference-KL term and apply dynamic sampling, skipping all-success or all-failure groups and gathering 128 mixed trajectories (16 groups) before launching training of that step. RAFT++ as baseline. As contrasting offpolicy baseline we also consider RAFT++ (Xiong et al., 2025), which can be viewed as vanilla multistep GRPO with the same per-trajectory normalizer 1/τi, but with group normalization disabled and the group-relative advantage replaced by success filter (r > 0). Only successful trajectories contribute gradient, so RAFT++ effectively performs behavior cloning on rolling buffer of positives and provides no contrastive signal on below-average trajectories. Decoupled importance sampling is still applied to keep the off-policy gradient unbiased.

sections.title: 4.1 Setup
sections.content: Environment. We train and test under the protocol from WebGym (Bai et al., 2026). We picked WebGym because it is the largest open multi-step visual web-agent training environment to date: roughly 290k training tasks across 128k real-world websites in three difficulty levels (Easy, Medium, Hard), evaluated on 1,167 task OOD test split whose websites do not appear in training. The scale of the OOD test split (1,167 tasks across held-out websites) is what makes single-seed comparisons informative here. WebGyms own analysis reports that two runs differing in earlytraining trajectory dynamics nonetheless converge to overlapping final test performance, with error bars across runs small enough that single-seed comparisons are informative (Bai et al., 2026, Figure 8). We inherit this property and report single-seed results throughout, focusing reporting budget on algorithm-level ablations rather than seed sweeps. Rewards are binary, produced by WebGyms GPT-4o rubric evaluator. Following WebGym, we use the coordinate-based action space {click, type, scroll, go_back, navigate, ANSWER} on raw screenshots, with per-difficulty horizons of (10, 20, 30) steps. The Easy-difficulty horizon k=10 is the WebGym recommended trade-off between truncation and rollout cost, and our constant 1/k fix uses k=10 throughout. All hyperparameter details are in Section C.5. Model Variants. We use two Qwen3-VL-8B Instruct (Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct) and variants: Thinking (Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking), both with the additive memory prompt template shown in Section C.4. At each step the policy sees only the previous screenshot and its previous response. For the Thinking variant the previous steps thinking tokens are hidden from history, following standard practice (Bai et al., 2026). Comparisons. REINFORCE style on-policy algorithms present type mismatch for an async framework that is not on-policy by design. We therefore compare to the closest off-policy baseline, RAFT++ (defined in Section 3.2). We run three configurations: WebGym: prior synchronous RL pipeline with the Filtered BC objective of Bai et al. (2026), the published WebGym numbers. AsyncWebRL-RAFT++: our async framework with RAFT++ as the loss, the async compatible substitute for WebGyms sync REINFORCE. AsyncWebRL (full): our async framework with the full method from Section 3, namely multistep GRPO with decoupled importance sampling and the constant 1/k step-number normalizer.

Before comparing algorithms, we use AsyncWebRL-RAFT++ as like-for-like check against the published WebGym sync REINFORCE numbers (same loss Figure 3: Left: End to end training trajectory throughput. Right: Off-policyness during GRPO training for the Instruct (blue) and Thinking (red) Qwen3-VL-8B runs: mean and max of the per-token off-policy gap across training batch. family, async framework substituted for sync). AsyncWebRL-RAFT++ on Instruct reaches 39.3%, against 42.9% for the prior WebGym pipeline  (Table 1)  . The 3.6% gap is consistent with the importance sampling overhead any async framework has to pay, and the two methods land in the same neighborhood on the same loss family, with the gain reported above being net of this cost. Wall-clock cost moves the other way: AsyncWebRL produces about 3,100 training trajectories per hour on both variants against approximately 1,300 and 1,050 trajectories per hour for the synchronous WebGym baseline, 2.4 to 2.9 end-to-end speedup (Figure 3, left). The speedup also incorporates smaller engineering improvements over WebGym, including the distributed inference server and in-memory weight broadcast. Since WebGym (Bai et al., 2026) itself reports the fastest open-source rollout system for multi-step visual web agents to date, the 2.4 to 2.9 end-to-end speedup on top of it makes AsyncWebRL, to our knowledge, the fastest reported open multi-step RL framework for visual web agents. We qualify with reported because among open multi-step VLM RL frameworks in this category, only WebGym publishes throughput numbers. Off-policyness stays small. natural worry under fully async RL is that the off-policy gap might be large. AReaL (Fu et al., 2025), the closest async text reasoning analog, reports running with max staleness of η=4 for coding and η=8 for math. In our setting, with the max staleness set to η = 2, the mean per-token off-policy gap η stays near 1.5 and the max near 2.0 throughout training (Figure 3, right two panels), well below the cap, and GPUs stay busy throughout. Off-policyness is naturally smaller than in coding and math because web-agent responses are much shorter (Shen et al., 2025), giving higher rollout/train speed ratio and leaving few stale trajectories in the training batch.

sections.title: 4.3 Efficiency
sections.content: Removing 1/τi preserves performance and shortens trajectories. The constant 1/k replacement does not change what the policy can solve: test reward is essentially tied throughout training on both variants (Figure 4, first column). What changes is trajectory and per-step response length. Under the standard 1/τi normalizer the average trajectory is consistently longer, and the per-step response grows to around 240 tokens; the constant 1/k run uses fewer steps per trajectory and fewer tokens per step at matched test reward. Measured within each run (same hardware throughout), the trajectory contraction translates into per-step gradient-update time reduction of 1115% from the first 20 to the last 20 training steps under the constant 1/k fix, against only 35% under the standard 1/τi loss; the corresponding total per-step wall-clock reductions are 1819% vs. 45% (full table in Section A.5). The mechanism behind this, length-coupled memory drift, is analyzed in Section 5. Decoupled importance sampling halves the clip rate. separate ablation isolates the off-policy correction described in Section 3.2. We run coupled loss baseline that folds rollout staleness and current-update movement into single πθ/πbehave ratio with PPO-style clipping centered on the coupled ratio. The coupled run sits at roughly 2 the ϵ-clip-trigger rate of the decoupled run at start and improves training reward substantially more slowly (Figure 5). We therefore keep the decoupled factorization in every AsyncWebRL configuration above.

sections.title: 5.1 Main Analysis
sections.content: The 1/τi factor in multi-step GRPO is the step-level analog of the token-level normalizer Dr. GRPO (Liu et al., 2025b) identifies as biased: each trajectory enters the loss with total weight 1 regardless of length, so any token in long trajectory carries 1/τi share. In setting like ours where failure is dominated by horizon exhaustion rather than clearly wrong actions, this attenuates the penalty on exactly the trajectories the policy needs to learn to avoid. On the base model, only around 30% to 40% of rollouts succeed in the DAPO-filtered training batch, and failures average 12.5 steps against 5.1 for successes (a 2.4 gap) when RL starts. The short successful population is both rarer and shorter and cannot counterbalance the effect of long failed trajectories, so early in training, the policy is pulled toward longer rollouts (Figure 4, second column). This lengthening of trajectories then propagates into longer per-step responses through the prompt structure. Under the WebGym additivememory prompt against multi-step visual environment (Bai et al., 2026), each step appends to Memory, and each new screenshot exposes new information the policy can legitimately log, so Memory grows roughly linearly in step count. Under 1/τi the per-step Memory key count tracks the one-new-key-per-step diagonal, while under the constant 1/k fix it stays close to the basemodel baseline (Figure 7). Since Memory is part of the response, the larger step count drags perstep response length up by 33% (Figure 4, fourth column), and the entropy drop (third column) follows because the extra response length is mostly low-entropy Memory boilerplate (pertoken entropy annotations on representative responses in Section C.3). The schema itself also drifts: under 1/τi the typical trajectory rewrites Memory every step with generic placeholders like task_1 or current_step (34% of all observed key-occurrences are generic-slot, only 7% of trajectories preserve their key set end-to-end, only 36% of step pairs leave the Memory key set unchanged), while under the constant 1/k fix the policy declares small set of task-anchored keys at step 0 and holds them throughout (65%, 76%, generic slots drop to 11%, Table 2 and Figure 6; token-level last-step Figure 4: Effect of the 1/τi normalizer on GRPO training dynamics. Rows are the two Qwen3-VL-8B variants (top: Instruct, bottom: Thinking), columns are, from left to right, test reward, #steps per trajectory, per-token entropy, and #tokens per step. Test reward is essentially tied between the two losses, but the 1/τi run produces longer trajectories, longer per-step responses, and lower per-token entropy. Figure 5: Coupled vs. decoupled importance sampling under the async RL GRPO loss. From left to right: per update mean of training reward, fraction of tokens hit by the ϵ-clip, and fraction of tokens hit by the dual clip. GRPO No LN keys0 =keyslast No-edit step pairs Generic-slot keys 7% 65% 36% 76% 34% 11% Table 2: Schema signatures of Memory under GRPO with 1/τi vs. the constant 1/k fix (Instruct, additive prompt, first 30 steps per trajectory). Rows: fraction of trajectories with first-step and last-step key sets equal; fraction of consecutive step pairs with the same key set; fraction of key occurrences matching generic placeholder regex (e.g. task, current_step, search_query). responses in Sections C.1 and C.2). The constant 1/k fix restores full per-token weight on long failures, and every downstream symptom above (memory bloat, longer responses, lower entropy, generic schemas) disappears.

sections.title: 5.2 Ablations
sections.content: We run three ablations to confirm that the diagnosis above identifies the right cause. The first checks that closely related algorithm (RAFT++) exhibits the same pathology when it shares the same loss factor, ruling out group-relative advantage as the driver. The second checks that the cause lives in the loss rather than the prompt, by trying to fix the symptom from the prompt side and observing that it does not work. The third checks that the effect scales as the mechanism predicts when we make long failures even longer. RAFT++ exhibits the same pathology, more mildly. RAFT++ also normalizes per-trajectory loss by 1/τi, and the diagnosis predicts the same memory bloat in kind. Figure 7 (Instruct row) confirms it: the RAFT++ per-step Memory key-count curve drifts upward in the same direction as GRPO with 1/τi, closely shadowing the GRPO lengthnorm curve on Easy successful trajectories and sitting well above the constant-1/k run. The effect is muted on failure trajectories, consistent with RAFT++s success-conditioned filter (r > 0) providing no direct gradient on failed rollouts: the same length-coupled attenuation is present in the loss but only propagates through the kept-positives. The matched per-step Add/Del/Mod edit-op decomposition (Section A.3) tells the same story at smaller magnitude than full GRPO. RAFT++ and GRPO differ in nearly every other respect, yet both show the same length-coupled drift whenever they share the 1/τi factor. The factor itself, not the surrounding algorithm, is the driver. Prompt-level intervention fails. If the loss is what drives the pathology, not the prompt, then intervening at the prompt level should not help. Swapping the additive-memory prompt for compressive one that explicitly instructs the model to compress Memory at every step, while keeping 1/τi in the loss, leaves per-step Add/Del/Mod rates elevated relative to the Base run (Table 3, GRPO, compressive). The prompt change does not reach the cause. Scaled horizons amplify the effect. The mechanism predicts that anything making long failures even longer should make the symptoms worse. Scaling per-difficulty horizons from 10/20/30 to Figure 6: Memory at step 4 of one representative 10/20/30-horizon rollout per checkpoint. 1/τi accumulates verbose generic keys; the constant 1/k fix re-keys Memory to task sub-questions. Figure 7: Trajectory-mean number of Memory JSON keys per agent step, split by outcome (Success, Failure) and difficulty (Easy, Medium). 1/τi tracks the one-new-key-per-step diagonal; the constant 1/k fix stays close to Base. 20/40/60 while keeping 1/τi does exactly that, and we see the predicted amplification in Figure 7: the GRPO (length norm, 20/40/60) curve tracks the one-new-key-per-step diagonal across both Easy and Medium panels, exceeding the default 10/20/30 length-norm run.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We presented AsyncWebRL, fully asynchronous multi-step RL framework for visual web agents. An everlasting rollout pool and lightweight screenshot handling give 2.42.9 end-to-end speedup over the fastest prior open pipeline; replacing 1/τi with constant 1/k then contracts trajectories at matched reward for further 1.80 per-step speedup. decoupled importance-sampling correction sets new open SOTA on WebGyms OOD split (45.4% vs. 42.9%).

sections.title: A Additional Analyses
sections.content: This appendix collects supporting analyses for the main-text results. Section A.1 reports learningrate ablation on RAFT++. Section A.2 unpacks the GRPO-vs-RAFT++ gap with entropy and cliprate diagnostics. Section A.3 provides the per-step Memory Add/Del/Mod table referenced by the Section 5 ablations. Section A.4 checks batch-size sensitivity. A.1 RAFT++ Learning Rate Two RAFT++ runs on Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct that share dataset, batch size (b=120), and every other hyperparameter except the learning rate (5106 vs. 1105) move in opposite directions on training and held-out reward (Figure 8): the smaller-LR run reaches higher peak training reward but markedly lower peak test success rate; the larger-LR run gives up some training fit and gains substantially on the test set. We do not have controlled experiment to attribute the flip causally, but the pattern is consistent with the RAFT++ objective behaving like behavior cloning on slowlychanging positives buffer, where many small gradient steps can over-fit the kept successful trajectories. This observation motivates our choice of the higher learning rate (1 105) for the Thinking RAFT++ baseline reported in Table 5. A.2 Why GRPO Outperforms RAFT++ RAFT++ falls short of GRPO on both variants. We attribute this primarily to RAFT++ providing no contrastive signal on below-average rollouts: its > 0 filter only pulls mass onto successful trajectories, while GRPOs group-normalized advantage simultaneously pushes mass down on below-average trajectories within the same group. The entropy gap on Instruct in Figure 9 is consistent with this picture: RAFT++s entropy collapses from 0.057 to 0.010 while GRPOs settles near 0.025, and ϵclip rates are essentially identical ( 0.3% in both runs), so the gap is not driven by clipping mechanics. We do not run controlled ablation that isolates the contrastive signal from other differences between the two losses. A.3 Memory Edit-Op Rates Table 3 reports per-step Add/Del/Mod edit-op rates on the agents Memory field, split by trajectory outcome, for the algorithm-prompt-loss combinations studied in Section 5. The matched per-step MemFigure 8: Learning-rate ablation on RAFT++ (Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, b=120, off-policy = 2, no KL). From left to right: held-out test success rate (the larger-LR run wins by wide margin at the peak), training reward, where the trend reverses, pre-clipping gradient L2 norm (consistently bounded for the larger LR), and per-token policy entropy, which decays faster under the larger LR. The train/test flip suggests low-LR RAFT++ overfits the filtered positives buffer, noisier optimization generalizes better. Figure 9: GRPO vs. RAFT++ on Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct. Left: training reward per optimizer step. Middle: per-token policy entropy averaged over the loss mask, the step-zero offset is induced by the conditional vs. unconditional denominator (see text). Right: fraction of tokens hitting the ϵ-clip, both runs sit near 0.3%, ruling out clip mechanics as the driver of the entropy gap. ory key-count curves are plotted in Figure 7; this table reports the operation-level decomposition, and tells four qualitative stories. RAFT++ edits Memory more aggressively than Base, consistent with the shared 1/τi factor pushing the policy toward longer memory schemas. GRPO with 1/τi edits even more aggressively than RAFT++, because the group-relative advantage adds direct negative gradient on below-average trajectories that RAFT++s success-conditioned filter does not provide. The compressive prompt under 1/τi brings rates down from full additive GRPO but stays above Base, indicating that prompt-level intervention only mitigates the loss-driven effect rather than removing it. Finally, replacing 1/τi with the constant 1/k, even using the additive memory prompt, brings the rates back to roughly Base levels (and below on Success), confirming that the loss aggregation is the root cause analyzed in Section 5. A.4 Batch Size Sensitivity We chose consumer batch size of 128 for the canonical Instruct GRPO run (Section C.5). To check whether our conclusions depend on this choice, we re-ran the same configuration with batch size 32, holding learning rate, group size, off-policy lag cap, and all other hyperparameters fixed (Figure 10). The small-batch run improves faster early (reaching 0.38 test reward several hours ahead of batch-128, since four-times-higher optimizerupdate rate cashes in when the gradient signal is large) but intersects with the batch-128 run around 10 hours (reaching 0.40), so over the full training horizon the choice is essentially neutral on test reward. The early gain comes with visibly higher per-iteration variance: batch-32 training reward, entropy, and steps per trajectory all swing within wider envelope than batch-128 at every wall-clock time. Crucially, the qualitative behaviors analyzed in Section 5 (entropy collapse, falling steps per trajectory, rising tokens per step) still reproduce at batch size 32, so the length-coupled memory drift mechanism is property of the loss shape rather than of the batch-size choice. The canonical batch size of 128 is therefore sensible default, but the main qualitative claims of the paper do not depend on it. A.5 Within-Run Per-Step Time Contraction To quantify per-step compute savings from the constant 1/k fix without cross-run hardware confounds, we report within-run ratios: the average time per training step over the last 20 steps divided by the average over the first 20  (Table 4)  . Each run executes on the same hardware throughout, so any change reflects the policy contracting (or expanding) its trajectories over training. We report two measurements. The first is the gradient-update Table 3: Per-step edit operations on Memory (instruct prompt, first 30 steps), split by trajectory outcome. From left: test reward, training reward, policy entropy, average steps per trajectory, and average response tokens per step. time only (forward, backward, optimizer), which the trainer records directly for every training step. The second is the total time elapsed per training step, also including rollout collection, evaluation, and checkpointing. For the total time, we skip small number of initial steps whose recorded timing reflects setup or resume overhead rather than steady-state training; for one of the Thinking 1/k fragments this means the first window starts few dozen training steps in. Table 4: Within-run ratio of last-20 to first-20 per-step time. Lower is more contraction. Run Gradient Total Instruct, 1/τi Instruct, 1/k Thinking, 1/τi Thinking, 1/k 0.97 0.85 0.95 0.89 0.96 0.81 0.95 0.82 Both measurements agree. Under the standard 1/τi loss, per-step time is essentially flat over the run (0.950.97): the policys trajectory-length distribution drifts slowly enough that per-step cost stays roughly constant. Under the constant 1/k fix, per-step time contracts noticeably within single run (0.810.89, an 1119% reduction from start to end of training). This is the within-run signature of the trajectory contraction analyzed in Section 5: as training proceeds, the 1/k policy emits shorter trajectories and fewer tokens per step, so each gradient update consumes less compute.

sections.title: B Implementation Details
sections.content: This appendix documents the system, loss, prompt, and hyperparameter details needed to reproduce our runs. The System subsection below describes the lightweight screenshot handling promised in Section 3.1. Section B.2 defines the dual-clip variant used by every AsyncWebRL GRPO run. Section C.4 provides the full agent prompt template (Instruct and Thinking share it). Section C.5 lists optimizer, loss, rollout, and hardware-allocation settings for all three main-table runs. B.1 System Lightweight screenshot handling. Different from WebGym, which embeds raw image tensors directly into each trajectory dictionary and ships the entire bundle through the shared RPC object store, AsyncWebRL stores all screenshots in dedicated in-memory actor and routes only lightweight references between rollout workers and the trainer, as shown in Figure 11. This redesign is forced by the multi-step web-agent setting: each trajectory carries tens of high-resolution screenshots, and at hundreds of concurrent rollouts the baseline pipeline pushes well over 100 GiB of pixel tensors through the object store per training step. Once the object store exceeds its memory budget, the framework spills to disk and every downstream operation, trajectory deserialization, RPC dispatch, and tensor localization, degrades by orders of magnitude. By keeping image tensors out of the RPC payload entirely, AsyncWebRL confines the screenshot footprint to single bounded actor, the trainer fetches Figure 11: Lightweight Screenshot Handling. Compared to WebGym, which serializes every screenshot through the shared RPC object store and spills to disk under concurrent rollouts, AsyncWebRL keeps all image tensors in dedicated in-memory actor and routes only lightweight references through RPC, eliminating the per-step object-store bottleneck. only the slices it needs at gradient-update time and immediately releases them once the optimizer step completes. B.2 Optimization There are two considerations of algorithm design that we adopt in our experiments. Dual-clipped GRPO loss. Let (ρ) ˆAi,t denote the inner min argument of Equation (1). This advantage-based clipping framework bounds the surrogate objective when ˆAi,t 0, but fails to do so when ˆAi,t < 0 and ρ 1 + ϵ: in this regime the inner min selects the unclipped term ρ ˆAi,t, which is unbounded below in ρ. single token assigned low probability by the rollout policy can therefore drive the per-token contribution arbitrarily negative. Following the dual-clip extension of PPO (Ye et al., 2020), we cap (ρ) from below whenever the advantage is negative: fdual(ρ) = (ρ), (cid:104) max (ρ), ˆAi,t (cid:105) , ˆAi,t 0, ˆAi,t < 0, (2) with constant > 1. The dual-clipped GRPO loss is obtained by substituting fdual(ρ) for (ρ) in Equation (1), providing an absolute lower bound on the per-token contribution and eliminating the tail risk introduced by rare low-probability tokens with exploded importance sampling ratios. On not penalizing overlong trajectories specially. trajectory that exhausts its horizon is marked as failure. DAPO (Yu et al., 2025) notes that this loses some potentially useful signal. We accept the loss: given the horizon, trajectory that exhausted it means some of its actions were suboptimal, at least when another trajectory in the same group did succeed. Actions like goback that appear more often in long trajectories are something we want the policy to take only when necessary, not on every long rollout. When every trajectory in the group fails by exhausting the horizon, either those goback-style actions were genuinely unhelpful, or the horizon itself is too tight; either way, treating overlong rollouts as failures is the correct default.

Section C.1 and Section C.2 contrast last-step responses produced by the 1/τi run against the constant 1/k run on the two Qwen3VL-8B variants, making the generic-slot memory schema versus task-anchored schema split visible at the token level. Section C.3 overlays the same responses with per-token entropy annotations, which lets the reader see directly that the extra tokens emitted under 1/τi sit in low-entropy boilerplate regions rather than in decision-bearing positions. The upper sub-table lists settings shared across all three runs; the lower sub-table lists the settings that differ. RAFT++ uses consumer batch size of 120 rather than 128 because we find that using 6 GPUs for training is more speed-optimal for RAFT++, while because we use group size of 8 we naturally use consumer batch size of 128 for GRPO. This is purely scheduling choice; no other hyperparameter was retuned. The Thinking-variant GRPO runs share the same prompt template (Section C.4) and the same loss, optimizer, and rollout settings as the Instruct GRPO runs; only the base model differs (Qwen3-VL-8BThinking) and, at runtime, the previous steps thinking tokens are hidden from history. The Thinkingvariant RAFT++ run additionally uses higher learning rate (1 105), single update epoch per batch, and relaxed off-policy lag cap of 4. </tool_call> # Response format Response format for every step: 1) Memory: facts you would like to memorize for future actions in json format. Include the current step. 2) Progress: Decompose the task into subtasks and what has been finished so far with json format. Include progress of the current step. 3) Intention: clearly state which subtask you're working on at this step with the json key. 4) Action: short sentence describing what to do in the UI to accomplish the next subtask. 5) single <tool_call>...</tool_call> block containing only the JSON: {"name": <functionname>, " arguments": <argsjsonobject>}. Rules: Output exactly in the order: Memory, Progress, Intention, Action, <tool_call>. You MUST use json format for the Memory and Progress parts. Example Task: "Search and compare the prices and locations of product 1 and product 2 on Amazon ." Example of Memory json format: {"Price of product 1": "10.00", "Location of product 1": "10.00", "Price of product 2": "12.00"}. Example of Progress json format: {"Go to Amazon. com": "finished", "Search for price of product 1": "finished", "Search for location of product 1": " finished", "Search for price of product 2": " finished", "Search for location of product 2": "not finished", "Compare product 1 and product 2": " not finished"}. Example of Intention json key format: "Search for location of product 2". - (default, additive memory prompt) You CANNOT modify previous Memory. Only append to it. - (ablation, compressive memory prompt) Each step, you should COMPRESS previous memory by identifying only those relevant. You CAN modify Progress from previous conversation to further decompose the task and guide your next action. For example, if the previous assistant message specifies Progress: {"Go to Amazon.com": " finished", "Search for product 1": "finished", " Search for product 2": "not finished", "Compare product 1 and 2": "not finished"}, You should further decompose "Search for product 1" and "Search for product 2" into "Search for price of product 1" and "Search for location of product 1", and "Search for price of product 2" and "Search for location of product 2".
